subject: family_law
topic_heading: Spousal Support — Entitlement, Amount, and Duration
subtopic_heading: Entitlement Grounds Under FLA and Divorce Act
fact_pattern: The wife gave up her career as a licensed engineer to care for the couple's three children for 12 years. On separation, she has no income and limited prospects for re-entering the engineering profession at her former level. She seeks spousal support.
call_of_question: What ground of spousal support entitlement does this fact pattern most clearly engage?

options.A: Contractual — the wife must show a written agreement obligating the husband to pay support.
options.B: Non-compensatory (need-based) — because the wife currently has no income.
options.C: Compensatory — the wife suffered economic disadvantage from the marriage and its breakdown by foregoing her engineering career to assume primary child care responsibilities, engaging FLA s.33(8)(a) and the Moge v Moge compensatory framework.
options.D: No entitlement — a spouse cannot claim support if she voluntarily left the workforce.

explanation: A spouse who sacrificed a career to care for children during the marriage has suffered an economic disadvantage caused by the marriage and its breakdown. This is the paradigm case of compensatory support recognized in Moge v Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813, and reflected in FLA s.33(8)(a) (spouse left employment to care for children or home) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)(a)(b). The primary basis here is compensatory, not need-based or contractual.
why_A_wrong: Contractual entitlement requires a pre-existing agreement to pay support. This fact pattern involves a statutory entitlement, not a contractual one.
why_B_wrong: While the wife has need (no income), the predominant basis is compensatory: the economic disadvantage flows from the division of labour during the marriage. Non-compensatory support is the better fit where the disadvantage is not tied to the marriage (see Bracklow).
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Career sacrifice for child care is the paradigm compensatory support case under Moge v Moge and FLA s.33(8)(a).
why_D_wrong: Voluntarily leaving the workforce to care for children is precisely the situation FLA s.33(8)(a) addresses. There is no 'voluntary choice' bar to compensatory support in these circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Spousal Support — Entitlement, Amount, and Duration
subtopic_heading: Entitlement Grounds Under FLA and Divorce Act
fact_pattern: Husband and wife both worked throughout their 20-year marriage and maintained equal incomes. Near the end of the marriage, the wife developed a serious illness that left her permanently unable to work. The illness was not caused by or related to the marriage. On separation, the wife seeks spousal support.
call_of_question: What ground of spousal support entitlement most appropriately applies?

options.A: Compensatory — because the marriage caused the illness.
options.B: No entitlement — a spouse cannot claim support for a medical condition unrelated to the marriage.
options.C: Non-compensatory — under Bracklow v Bracklow, 1999 SCC, support may be awarded on a non-compensatory basis where the spouse has a need arising from the marriage and its breakdown, grounded in the relationship of mutual interdependency, even where the marriage did not cause the economic disadvantage.
options.D: Contractual — the wife needs a pre-existing support agreement to establish entitlement.

explanation: Where there is no economic disadvantage caused by the marriage (the illness was not marriage-related), compensatory support does not apply. Bracklow v Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420 recognized that support may also be awarded on a non-compensatory basis, reflecting the social obligation and mutual interdependency arising from marriage. The wife's need resulting from a disability arising near the end of a long marriage may engage this ground.
why_A_wrong: The illness was not caused by or related to the marriage, so there is no economic disadvantage from the marriage to compensate. The compensatory basis requires a causal link to the marriage.
why_B_wrong: Bracklow expressly recognizes non-compensatory support based on need and mutual interdependency, even where the marriage did not cause the disadvantage. A medical disability creating need is not an automatic bar to support.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Bracklow non-compensatory support applies where need arises from the breakdown of a marriage relationship of interdependency.
why_D_wrong: Contractual entitlement requires a pre-existing agreement. The Bracklow non-compensatory ground is a statutory, not contractual, basis.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Spousal Support — Entitlement, Amount, and Duration
subtopic_heading: Entitlement Grounds Under FLA and Divorce Act
fact_pattern: The parties were married for four years and have no children. Both worked throughout the marriage in similar professional roles. On separation, the husband earns $200,000 per year and the wife earns $190,000 per year. The wife applies for spousal support.
call_of_question: Is the wife entitled to spousal support in these circumstances?

options.A: Yes — any spouse may apply for and receive support on separation regardless of need or the nature of the marriage.
options.B: Yes — the $10,000 income difference alone creates an entitlement to support.
options.C: Likely no — given the short marriage, absence of children, and near-equal incomes, there is no apparent compensatory basis (no career sacrifice or economic disadvantage from the marriage) and no strong non-compensatory basis (no significant need or dependency); a court would likely find no entitlement.
options.D: Yes — a spouse who earns less than the other is always entitled to receive spousal support.

explanation: Entitlement to spousal support requires a basis in compensation, need/interdependency, or contract. A short marriage (4 years) with no children, equal careers, and near-equal incomes does not typically give rise to compensatory support (no career sacrifice or economic disadvantage caused by the marriage) or non-compensatory support (no significant need or dependency, given the high income). The income difference of $10,000 is not itself determinative of entitlement.
why_A_wrong: Entitlement to spousal support must be established on one of the recognized grounds (compensatory, non-compensatory, contractual). A right to apply is not the same as a right to receive support.
why_B_wrong: An income difference alone does not create spousal support entitlement. The analysis requires examining whether the income disparity is attributable to the marriage and its roles, or reflects pre-existing or unrelated factors.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Short marriage, no children, equal careers, and near-equal incomes produce no identifiable compensatory or non-compensatory ground for support.
why_D_wrong: An income differential does not automatically create an entitlement to support. The court must find an applicable ground of entitlement before addressing amount and duration.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Spousal Support — Entitlement, Amount, and Duration
subtopic_heading: Entitlement Grounds Under FLA and Divorce Act
fact_pattern: A court has found that the wife is entitled to spousal support. The husband argues that the finding of entitlement automatically determines both the amount and duration of support.
call_of_question: Does a finding of entitlement to spousal support automatically determine the amount and duration of the support award?

options.A: Yes — once entitlement is established, the court must award the maximum amount specified by the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines.
options.B: Yes — a finding of entitlement automatically entitles the claimant to lifelong spousal support.
options.C: No — establishing entitlement is only the first step; amount and duration are then determined separately using factors under the FLA or Divorce Act, with the SSAG serving as a non-binding reference guide; the court may award a nominal amount or time-limited support even where entitlement exists.
options.D: Yes — once entitled, the court must award an amount equal to one-half the difference in the spouses' incomes.

explanation: Spousal support analysis has three distinct stages: (1) entitlement, (2) amount, and (3) duration. A finding of entitlement does not predetermine the other two. The SSAG are non-binding guidelines that assist in calculating amount and duration but do not mandate any particular outcome. The court retains discretion and may award a nominal or time-limited amount even where entitlement is clear.
why_A_wrong: The SSAG are non-binding advisory guidelines. Even if entitlement is established, the court is not required to award the SSAG maximum. Amount is a separate and discretionary determination.
why_B_wrong: Duration is a separate question from entitlement. A finding of entitlement does not automatically lead to lifelong support; duration depends on the length of the marriage, role assumed, and other factors.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Entitlement, amount, and duration are three separate stages; entitlement does not predetermine the others.
why_D_wrong: No such automatic formula exists. The income-splitting approach described in Option D does not reflect the law; amount is determined by multiple factors and the SSAG ranges, not a fixed formula.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Spousal Support — Entitlement, Amount, and Duration
subtopic_heading: Entitlement Grounds Under FLA and Divorce Act
fact_pattern: The wife received spousal support for five years following separation. Her financial circumstances then improved significantly when she remarried a wealthy spouse, leaving her with no financial need. The husband brings an application to terminate the support order.
call_of_question: What legal principle governs whether a spousal support order may be varied or terminated?

options.A: The husband may unilaterally stop making payments once the wife remarries, without a court order.
options.B: Spousal support cannot be varied once ordered unless both parties consent in writing.
options.C: A material change in circumstances since the original order was made allows either party to bring a variation application; the wife's remarriage and significant improvement in financial circumstances may constitute a material change justifying reduction or termination of support.
options.D: No variation is available during the first 10 years of a spousal support order.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.17 and FLA s.37, a court may vary or rescind a spousal support order where there has been a material change in circumstances since the order was made. The wife's remarriage and the elimination of her financial need are circumstances that a court would likely consider to constitute a material change, and the husband may succeed in obtaining a termination or reduction of the support order. Unilateral cessation of payments without a court order is not permitted.
why_A_wrong: A court order is required to vary or terminate a spousal support order. Unilateral cessation of payments exposes the husband to enforcement proceedings, including contempt.
why_B_wrong: Variation does not require mutual consent. Either party may bring a variation application based on a material change in circumstances under Divorce Act s.17 or FLA s.37.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Material change in circumstances is the statutory test for variation; remarriage and elimination of need are classic examples.
why_D_wrong: No such 10-year moratorium on variation exists in either the FLA or the Divorce Act. Variation may be sought at any time on demonstration of a material change.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Spousal Support — Material Change in Circumstances
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 37; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: Patricia and her husband Colin separated after 11 years of marriage with no dependent children. The court has already determined that Patricia is entitled to compensatory spousal support. Colin earns $130,000 per year and Patricia earns $52,000. Both lawyers refer to the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines to determine the appropriate amount and duration of support. Colin's lawyer argues the SSAG ranges are mandatory and the court must award support within the stated range.
call_of_question: What is the correct legal status of the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) in Ontario?

options.A: The SSAG are binding on Ontario courts under the Divorce Act and the FLA; the court must stay within the calculated range.
options.B: The SSAG are not law; they are advisory only. Courts frequently consult them but are not required to follow them, and may depart from the SSAG range in appropriate circumstances.
options.C: The SSAG are binding only in the Superior Court of Justice, not on consent orders.
options.D: The SSAG replace the entitlement analysis; if the SSAG range produces a non-zero result, entitlement is automatically established.

explanation: The Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines are advisory only. They were developed as a non-legislative tool to promote consistency and predictability in spousal support quantum and duration. Courts are not required to follow the SSAG but regularly consult them as a useful reference. Departure from the range is permitted where the circumstances of the case warrant a different result. The SSAG have not been enacted as legislation and have no binding statutory force.
why_A_wrong: The SSAG have not been enacted under the Divorce Act or the FLA. They carry no statutory authority and are advisory only. Courts are not bound to stay within the SSAG range.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The SSAG are advisory, not binding; courts consult them for guidance on quantum and duration but may depart from the ranges in appropriate cases.
why_C_wrong: The SSAG are not binding in any Ontario court, including the Superior Court of Justice. They are advisory across all levels of court for both contested and consent proceedings.
why_D_wrong: The SSAG apply only after entitlement is established through the compensatory, non-compensatory, or contractual analysis. The SSAG do not determine entitlement; they assist with quantum and duration after entitlement is found.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2

tested_concepts: SSAG apply to quantum and duration not entitlement

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Variation Under Divorce Act s.17 — Material Change in Circumstances
fact_pattern: Marcus and Diana divorced five years ago. The court ordered Marcus to pay Diana $3,000 per month in spousal support indefinitely. Since the order was made, Marcus has been involuntarily laid off and now earns $40,000 per year, down from $150,000. He brings a variation application. Diana argues the original order cannot be varied because the court labeled it 'final.'
call_of_question: On a variation application under Divorce Act s.17, what must Marcus demonstrate to obtain a reduction in his spousal support obligation?

options.A: Marcus must demonstrate that he made a bona fide attempt to find equivalent employment and failed, and that his income loss will last forever.
options.B: Marcus must show a material change in circumstances — a change that, had it been known at the time of the original order, would likely have resulted in a different order; his involuntary job loss and income reduction from $150,000 to $40,000 would likely satisfy this test.
options.C: Marcus cannot vary the order because it was labeled 'final'; only a consent order or an appeal may alter a final support order.
options.D: Marcus must show that Diana's needs have decreased, not that his ability to pay has changed.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.17, a court may vary a spousal support order where there has been a 'change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances' of either spouse since the order was made. The Supreme Court in Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920 confirmed that the change must be material — meaning that if it had been known at the time, it would likely have produced a different result. An involuntary loss of employment reducing income from $150,000 to $40,000 is a paradigm material change, regardless of whether the original order was labeled 'final.'
why_A_wrong: There is no requirement under DA s.17 that the income loss be permanent or that the payor demonstrate inability to find equivalent work before bringing a variation application. A significant income reduction is sufficient to trigger the material change analysis.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under DA s.17 and Leskun, a material change in circumstances — such as a dramatic involuntary income reduction — is the proper basis for a variation application, regardless of the 'final' label on the order.
why_C_wrong: The label 'final' does not bar a variation application under DA s.17. A spousal support order remains variable upon proof of a material change in circumstances. Only agreements specifically contracted out of variation rights would create a bar, and even then, courts retain jurisdiction.
why_D_wrong: Section 17 allows variation based on a change in the 'means' of either spouse — including the payor's ability to pay. Marcus need not show that Diana's needs have decreased; his own income reduction is a valid basis for seeking a reduction.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Spousal Support — Material Change in Circumstances
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Termination of Support — Remarriage, Death, Cohabitation, Self-Sufficiency
fact_pattern: Elena receives spousal support of $2,500 per month under a consent order that is silent on termination events. She has been cohabiting with a new partner for two years in a marriage-like relationship. Her new partner has a high income and contributes substantially to household expenses. Elena's income has not changed. Her former husband applies to terminate support.
call_of_question: Which legal principle is most relevant to the former husband's application to terminate Elena's spousal support?

options.A: Cohabitation with a new partner automatically terminates a spousal support order by operation of law in Ontario.
options.B: The consent order cannot be varied or terminated because Elena and her former husband agreed to it; the court has no jurisdiction to interfere.
options.C: Cohabitation in a marriage-like relationship may constitute a material change in circumstances under DA s.17 if it results in a change in Elena's means or needs; the court considers the economic impact of the new relationship, not the mere fact of cohabitation.
options.D: Spousal support is only terminated by remarriage or death; cohabitation has no legal effect on a support order.

explanation: Cohabitation with a new partner does not automatically terminate spousal support in Ontario. However, under Divorce Act s.17, cohabitation may constitute a material change in circumstances if it affects the recipient's means or needs. If the new partner contributes significantly to living expenses, reducing Elena's financial need, the court may reduce or terminate support. The inquiry focuses on the economic impact of cohabitation, not cohabitation per se. Under the Family Law Act s.37(2)(c), a support obligation is also affected by a spouse's co-habitation.
why_A_wrong: There is no automatic termination of spousal support upon cohabitation in Ontario by operation of law. The court must assess whether the cohabitation constitutes a material change affecting the recipient's needs or means.
why_B_wrong: Courts retain jurisdiction to vary consent orders upon a material change in circumstances. A consent order is not irrevocable; DA s.17 applies equally to consent-based and court-imposed orders.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Cohabitation triggers the material change analysis under DA s.17; the court assesses the economic impact on Elena's needs and means, not the bare fact of cohabitation.
why_D_wrong: While remarriage and death are common termination events, they are not the only ones. Cohabitation can constitute a material change that justifies variation or termination, depending on its economic impact on the recipient.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Termination of Spousal Support — Events After Order
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 37(2)(c)

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: A spousal support order was made two years ago requiring Roy to pay $2,000/month. Roy lost his job eight months ago but delayed bringing his variation application. He now asks the court to reduce his support retroactively to the date of his job loss and to cancel the arrears that have accumulated since that date.
call_of_question: What principles govern a court's discretion to make a retroactive variation of spousal support under the Divorce Act?

options.A: Retroactive variation is not available under the Divorce Act; all reductions take effect only from the date of the court order.
options.B: The court has discretion to make a retroactive variation and must do so to the date of the triggering event, cancelling all arrears from that date as a matter of course.
options.C: The court has discretion under DA s.17 to make a retroactive variation; relevant considerations include the reason for the delay in bringing the application, whether the recipient was given notice of the changed circumstances, prejudice to the recipient, and the conduct of the payor.
options.D: Retroactive reduction of spousal support arrears is only possible by consent agreement; a court cannot unilaterally cancel accumulated arrears.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.17(1), a court may vary a support order and the variation may be retroactive. The court exercises discretion in determining the retroactive start date. Relevant factors include: (1) the payor's reason for the delay in bringing the application; (2) whether the recipient had notice of the changed circumstances and was able to adjust their lifestyle; (3) whether granting full retroactivity would cause hardship to the recipient; and (4) the overall fairness of the retroactive award. Courts do not automatically grant retroactivity to the triggering event; the circumstances dictate the appropriate retroactive date.
why_A_wrong: Retroactive variation is available under DA s.17. The court has jurisdiction to make an order effective from a date earlier than the variation application, in its discretion.
why_B_wrong: While retroactive variation is available, it is not mandatory to the date of the triggering event. The court exercises discretion based on delay, notice to the recipient, and potential prejudice. Full retroactivity is not automatic.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The court has discretion to grant retroactive variation, weighing delay, notice, prejudice to the recipient, and fairness — it does not automatically run to the triggering event date.
why_D_wrong: A court has jurisdiction under DA s.17 to retroactively vary or cancel arrears without requiring consent. While consent simplifies the process, it is not a prerequisite for retroactive judicial relief.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Retroactive Variation of Spousal Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(1); Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Interaction Between DA s.17 and SSAG for Variation
fact_pattern: A spousal support order was made five years ago awarding $1,800/month to the wife. At the time, the husband earned $100,000 and the wife earned $30,000. Today, following a promotion, the husband earns $180,000 and the wife's income remains $30,000. The wife brings a variation application and asks the court to increase support. Her lawyer argues the court should simply apply the current SSAG range to the new incomes and set support at the midpoint of that range.
call_of_question: When a court varies a spousal support order under DA s.17 due to changed income, how does the SSAG interact with the variation analysis?

options.A: On a variation application, the court must recalculate support de novo using the SSAG as if no prior order existed; the original order is irrelevant.
options.B: The SSAG cannot be used on variation applications; they apply only to initial orders.
options.C: On a variation, once a material change is established, the court may use the SSAG to help determine the appropriate new quantum; however, the SSAG are advisory only and the court retains discretion to depart from the range based on the specific circumstances, including the amount originally ordered and the parties' reliance on it.
options.D: On a variation, the court must award the exact SSAG midpoint for the new income figures; judicial discretion is displaced once the SSAG range is calculated.

explanation: On a DA s.17 variation application, the court first determines whether there has been a material change in circumstances. If so, it reconsiders quantum, and the SSAG serve as a useful — but non-binding — reference for the appropriate new amount. The SSAG do not displace judicial discretion; the court may consider the original order, the parties' expectations and reliance, and all other relevant circumstances. The court is not required to simply apply the current SSAG midpoint without reference to the history of the support obligation.
why_A_wrong: Variation is not a de novo hearing; the court starts from the existing order and determines whether the material change justifies a different quantum. The original order remains relevant context.
why_B_wrong: The SSAG apply on both initial orders and variation applications. Courts frequently use the SSAG to assess the appropriate quantum on variation, though they are not bound by the range.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The SSAG are advisory on variation applications; the court uses them as a reference for quantum but retains full discretion, including consideration of the original order and the parties' reliance.
why_D_wrong: The SSAG midpoint is not mandatory on variation or at any other stage. Courts retain discretion to award amounts within, above, or below the SSAG range based on the circumstances of the case.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Spousal Support — SSAG and Material Change
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021)

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: Renata and her husband Stefan separated after 18 years of marriage. They have two children, aged 10 and 14, for whom Renata has primary residence. The court has found Renata is entitled to both compensatory and non-compensatory spousal support. Stefan's lawyer argues the SSAG without-child formula applies because the parties have negotiated child support separately. Renata's lawyer argues the with-child formula should apply. The court must determine which SSAG formula governs the quantum analysis.
call_of_question: Which SSAG formula applies where the spouses have dependent children in the recipient's primary care?

options.A: The without-child formula always applies once child support has been separately determined; the existence of children is irrelevant to the spousal support quantum once child support is resolved.
options.B: The with-child formula applies where there are dependent children in the recipient spouse's primary care; this formula accounts for custodial arrangements and typically produces higher spousal support amounts than the without-child formula.
options.C: Neither formula applies; courts must set aside the SSAG entirely when children are involved and rely solely on FLA s. 33 factors.
options.D: The without-child formula applies because the parties have already negotiated a child support amount and the SSAG formulas are mutually exclusive.

explanation: The Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines provide two distinct formulas. The without-child formula applies to marriages without dependent children (or post-secondary adult children). The with-child formula applies where there are dependent children living primarily with the support recipient. The with-child formula is more complex — it takes into account the parenting arrangements, the child support paid, and typically produces a higher range of spousal support to reflect the combined economic impact of child-rearing responsibilities. The fact that child support has been separately agreed does not convert a with-child case into a without-child case; the formula depends on the presence of dependent children in the recipient's primary care.
why_A_wrong: Separate negotiation of child support does not render the children irrelevant to the spousal support formula. The SSAG with-child formula applies based on the custodial and parenting circumstances at the time of the spousal support determination.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The with-child SSAG formula applies where dependent children are in the primary care of the support recipient, and it typically produces higher support amounts than the without-child formula.
why_C_wrong: The SSAG are advisory but applicable to cases involving children. Courts do not set aside the SSAG entirely; they apply the with-child formula and may depart from the range if circumstances warrant.
why_D_wrong: The two formulas are not mutually exclusive in the sense that child support negotiations determine which formula applies. The applicable formula depends on the presence of dependent children in the recipient's primary care, not on whether child support has been separately resolved.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Three Grounds of Entitlement — Compensatory, Non-Compensatory, Contractual
fact_pattern: Omar and Leila divorced after a 9-year marriage. They have no children. Omar earned $160,000 per year as a lawyer throughout the marriage; Leila earned $65,000 as a graphic designer and continued working throughout the marriage. Leila did not take any career steps back for the marriage. Their separation agreement is silent on support. Leila now seeks spousal support.
call_of_question: Based on these facts, which ground of spousal support entitlement, if any, is most likely available to Leila?

options.A: Compensatory — because Omar earns more than Leila, creating an economic imbalance attributable to the marriage.
options.B: Non-compensatory — because Leila has a lower income and therefore a financial need relative to Omar.
options.C: Leila faces difficulty establishing any of the three entitlement grounds: there is no compensatory basis (she suffered no career disadvantage), likely insufficient non-compensatory basis (she has employment and income), and no contractual basis (the agreement is silent); a court would likely find no entitlement.
options.D: Contractual — the separation agreement creates an implied obligation to pay support.

explanation: The three recognized grounds for spousal support entitlement are: (1) compensatory — the claimant suffered an economic disadvantage from the marriage and its breakdown (Moge v. Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813); (2) non-compensatory — the claimant has a need arising from the relationship of mutual interdependence (Bracklow v. Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420); and (3) contractual. Leila has no compensatory claim (she maintained her career without sacrifice). Her non-compensatory claim is weak: she has steady employment and income of $65,000, so there is no significant need arising from marriage breakdown. The separation agreement creates no contractual entitlement. A court would likely find no entitlement on these facts.
why_A_wrong: Compensatory support requires an economic disadvantage caused by the marriage — such as a career sacrifice or forgone opportunity. An income differential alone does not establish compensatory entitlement if the lower-earning spouse did not sacrifice career opportunities for the marriage.
why_B_wrong: Non-compensatory (Bracklow) support requires a significant need arising from the breakdown of a relationship of mutual interdependency. Leila's continued employment and income of $65,000 make it difficult to establish the kind of significant need that grounds non-compensatory entitlement.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Without a compensatory basis (no career sacrifice), a persuasive non-compensatory basis (she has income and employment), or a contractual basis (the agreement is silent), Leila likely has no entitlement to spousal support.
why_D_wrong: A separation agreement that is silent on support does not create an implied obligation to pay. Contractual entitlement requires an express agreement or obligation. Silence in the agreement is not equivalent to an agreement to pay support.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Entitlement — Three Grounds Compensatory Non-Compensatory Contractual

tested_concepts: three grounds of spousal support entitlement

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: SSAG Without-Child Formula — Range and Duration
fact_pattern: Ingrid and Stefan separated after 12 years of marriage with no children. The court has found Ingrid is entitled to compensatory spousal support. Stefan earns $120,000 per year gross and Ingrid earns $45,000 per year gross. Both lawyers are using the SSAG without-child formula to advise on the likely range of support amounts and duration.
call_of_question: Under the SSAG without-child formula, how is the range of support amount generally calculated, and what is the general approach to duration?

options.A: The without-child formula calculates support as a fixed percentage of the payor's income regardless of the recipient's income.
options.B: The without-child formula generates a range of support amounts (low, mid, and high) based on a percentage of the gross income difference between the parties, typically 1.5–2% per year of marriage; duration is tied to the length of the marriage.
options.C: The without-child formula sets support at one-half the income difference between the spouses for an indefinite period.
options.D: The without-child formula applies only where the marriage was shorter than 10 years; at 12 years, the court must use the with-child formula regardless of the absence of children.

explanation: The SSAG without-child formula generates a range of spousal support amounts (low, mid, and high) based on a percentage of the gross income difference between the spouses. The percentage is calculated as 1.5% to 2% of the gross income difference per year of marriage, producing a range rather than a fixed amount. Duration under the without-child formula is generally tied to the length of the marriage, ranging from a low of half the years of marriage to a high of the full number of years, with an indefinite duration for long marriages (typically 20+ years or marriages over 5 years where the recipient is older). On Ingrid and Stefan's facts, the income difference is $75,000; the formula would generate a range reflecting approximately 12–24% of that difference as annual support.
why_A_wrong: The without-child formula is based on the gross income difference between the parties — not on a fixed percentage of the payor's income alone. Both incomes are relevant to the calculation.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The SSAG without-child formula applies 1.5–2% of gross income difference per year of marriage to generate a range; duration is tied to marriage length.
why_C_wrong: Sharing one-half the income difference indefinitely is not the SSAG without-child formula. The formula generates a range based on years of marriage and the income difference, with defined upper and lower bounds and a time-limited duration for most marriages.
why_D_wrong: The SSAG formula selection depends on whether there are dependent children in the recipient's primary care, not on the length of the marriage. With no children, the without-child formula applies regardless of marriage duration.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: SSAG Without-Child Formula — Amount and Duration
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2

tested_concepts: 1.5-2% gross income difference per year of marriage

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Self-Sufficiency Obligation Under DA s.15.2(6)(d) — FLA s.33(8)(h)
fact_pattern: A spousal support order in favour of Priya was made 3 years ago following a 7-year marriage with no children. Priya has a bachelor's degree in commerce. She has made no effort to find employment, claiming personal preference for not working. Her former husband Anil, who pays $2,000/month in support, brings a variation application arguing that Priya could be self-sufficient if she made reasonable efforts to find work.
call_of_question: What is the legal significance of Priya's failure to pursue self-sufficiency in the context of Anil's variation application?

options.A: Priya has no obligation to seek employment; once an order is made, the recipient's choices about employment are irrelevant.
options.B: A recipient spouse's failure to take reasonable steps toward self-sufficiency when capable of employment is a relevant factor on a variation application; courts may impute income to Priya based on her earning capacity and reduce or terminate support on that basis.
options.C: The court can only impute income to a payor, not to a recipient spouse.
options.D: Priya's support order cannot be varied because it was made less than five years ago.

explanation: Divorce Act s.15.2(6)(d) requires support orders to promote the economic self-sufficiency of each spouse within a reasonable period. FLA s.33(8)(h) similarly requires consideration of a spouse's ability to become self-sufficient. Courts have consistently held that a recipient who is capable of employment but makes no effort to become self-sufficient may have income imputed to them on a variation application, and support may be reduced or terminated. Three years after separation from a 7-year marriage with no children, a degree-holder who makes no employment effort is a strong candidate for income imputation and variation of the support order.
why_A_wrong: Spousal support is not intended to create permanent dependency where the recipient is capable of self-sufficiency. DA s.15.2(6)(d) expressly requires orders to promote self-sufficiency, and courts enforce this on variation applications.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A recipient's failure to pursue self-sufficiency when capable is a relevant factor on a variation application; courts may impute earning capacity and reduce or terminate support.
why_C_wrong: Courts regularly impute income to recipient spouses who fail to take reasonable steps toward self-sufficiency. Income imputation is not limited to payors — it applies to recipients as well where they are capable of earning income.
why_D_wrong: No minimum time period bars a variation application. A variation may be sought at any time upon demonstration of a material change in circumstances; a recipient's failure to take steps toward self-sufficiency can constitute such a material change.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Self-Sufficiency and Variation — DA s.15.2(6)(d)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)(d); Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8)(h)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Periods for Spousal Support Claims
subtopic_heading: Limitation Period for Spousal Support — FLA s.7(3) vs Divorce Act
fact_pattern: Violet and Conrad separated on January 1, 2022, without applying for divorce. As of February 1, 2026 — more than four years post-separation — Violet has not commenced any spousal support proceedings. She now wishes to claim spousal support under the Family Law Act. Conrad argues her claim is statute-barred.
call_of_question: What limitation period governs Violet's spousal support claim under the Family Law Act, and is her claim likely out of time?

options.A: The two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars Violet's claim; she had two years from the date of separation to commence proceedings.
options.B: There is no limitation period for spousal support claims; Violet may claim at any time.
options.C: Under the Family Law Act, a claim for spousal support (Part III) is subject to the general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date the claim was discovered; however, the date of discovery may be later than separation if Violet did not know or could not reasonably have known of her entitlement earlier.
options.D: The limitation period for FLA spousal support is six years from the date of separation; Violet's claim is not yet out of time.

explanation: Spousal support claims under the Family Law Act Part III are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002, which imposes a basic two-year limitation period running from the date the claim was discovered (s.4 of the Limitations Act, 2002). The discovery date may differ from the separation date — a claim is discovered when the person knew or ought to have known that the harm, loss, or damage occurred, and that a proceeding against the appropriate person would be an appropriate means to seek remedy. In practice, for spousal support, courts typically hold that the claim is discoverable from separation or shortly thereafter. If Violet's claim was discoverable in early 2022, it may be statute-barred by early 2024, four years later. Note: claims under the Divorce Act as corollary relief in divorce proceedings are not subject to this limitation period while divorce proceedings are pending.
why_A_wrong: While the two-year limitation is correct under the Limitations Act, 2002, Option A incorrectly treats separation as automatically the discovery date. The Limitations Act uses a discovery-based approach, and the actual discovery date must be assessed.
why_B_wrong: FLA spousal support claims are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 general two-year limitation period. There is no unlimited time to commence such claims.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA Part III support claims are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period from discovery; the claim may be statute-barred if discoverable in 2022 and commenced in 2026.
why_D_wrong: There is no six-year limitation period for FLA spousal support claims. The Limitations Act, 2002 basic limitation period is two years from discovery.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Spousal Support FLA vs Divorce Act
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

tested_concepts: DA claims not time-barred while pending

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Review vs Variation — Distinction and Purpose
fact_pattern: A spousal support order was made two years ago. The order includes the following clause: 'This order shall be subject to review on January 1, 2027 without the need to demonstrate a material change in circumstances.' The husband now seeks to terminate support, arguing he need not wait for the review date and can bring a variation application at any time.
call_of_question: What is the legal distinction between a review order and a variation application for spousal support, and how does the review clause affect the husband's ability to bring a variation application?

options.A: A review order and a variation application are legally identical; both require a material change in circumstances.
options.B: A review order allows the court to revisit support at the specified date without requiring the applicant to prove a material change in circumstances; a variation application under DA s.17 always requires proof of a material change; however, a review clause does not necessarily preclude a variation application where a material change has occurred before the review date.
options.C: A review clause permanently replaces the variation regime; the husband has no right to bring any application before the review date.
options.D: Review orders are not permitted under the Divorce Act; only variation applications are legally recognized.

explanation: A review order is a special kind of order that expressly allows the court to revisit the support obligation at a specified future date without the moving party needing to prove a material change in circumstances. This contrasts with a variation application under DA s.17, which requires the applicant to demonstrate a material change in the condition, means, needs, or circumstances of either spouse since the last order. A review clause does not necessarily bar a s.17 variation application before the review date — if a material change has occurred (e.g., significant income change, loss of employment), the husband may still bring a variation application. The review clause merely establishes an additional mechanism for revisiting support at the specified date without a material change threshold.
why_A_wrong: Review orders and variation applications differ precisely in the threshold required. A review order allows reconsideration at the review date without a material change; a variation application always requires proving a material change under DA s.17.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A review allows reconsideration at a set date without material change proof; variation requires material change; a review clause does not bar an earlier variation application if a material change has occurred.
why_C_wrong: A review clause does not permanently displace the variation regime or bar all applications before the review date. Courts have recognized that a payor may bring a variation application before a scheduled review date if a material change has occurred.
why_D_wrong: Review orders are a recognized form of spousal support order regularly made in Canadian family law. The Divorce Act's s.17 variation mechanism and review orders are both available tools; they serve different purposes and operate differently.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Review vs Variation — Distinct Mechanisms
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Fisher v. Fisher, 2008 ONCA 11

tested_concepts: review clause does not bar early variation
